title: Only True 007 Fans Can Guess These 8 Bond Films From Three Emoji
dek: From Sean Connery's tarantula to Daniel Craig's teary goodbye, we turned six decades of Bond into pure emoji chaos — good luck.

items.reveal_text: Skyfall isn't just the title — <cite index="59-3">Skyfall was the name of the childhood home of James Bond</cite>, and it's also where <cite index="60-13">Judi Dench's final Bond film—she had played M since 1995's GoldenEye</cite> came to its emotional end.

Fleming's Bond played baccarat, but the movie went a different route: <cite index="55-2,55-3">in the «Casino Royale» novel, Bond faces Le Chiffre at the casino game of «baccara chemin de fer», but in the film, baccara was replaced by Texas Hold'em poker</cite> — a much easier sell for modern audiences.

That legendary tank chase wasn't a model or CGI trick — <cite index="40-20">the Soviet tank, weighing 42 tons and capable of speeds of 35mph, was dubbed "Metal Mickey" by the crew</cite>, who drove it straight through a real city set built just for the stunt.

This was it for good: <cite index="69-2">the sequel to Spectre (2015), it is the fifth and final film to star Daniel Craig as the fictional British MI6 agent James Bond</cite>. Even the trivia stayed loyal to tradition — <cite index="68-9,68-10">Michael G. Wilson appears as a military officer at the SPECTRE party in Cuba, having made a cameo in every Bond movie since The Spy Who Loved Me (1977)</cite>.

Everybody wanted a piece of this one: <cite index="18-17">the film's release led to a number of promotional tie-in items, including a toy Aston Martin DB5 from Corgi Toys, which became the biggest-selling toy of 1964</cite>, right alongside a gold-painted magazine cover star.

The face you see in that very first gun barrel shot isn't even Bond's actor: <cite index="5-2,5-3">the actor in the sequence is not Sir Sean Connery, but stuntman Bob Simmons, and Connery didn't film the sequence until Thunderball (1965)</cite>.

That iconic ski-jump opener almost happened eight years earlier: <cite index="37-12,37-13">the sequence, where Bond skis off a cliff before opening a Union Jack parachute, was initially conceived for George Lazenby's Bond in On Her Majesty's Secret Service, but the money and technology to pull it off weren't there yet</cite>.

This one has the wildest what-if in Bond history: <cite index="26-6">Lazenby was offered a contract for seven films, but he was convinced by his agent that the secret agent would be archaic in the liberated 1970s, and left the series after this one release</cite>. One and done, forever.
